column3: Adams Sentinel and General Advertiser (1826-67) was a weekly newspaper published in Gettysburg, Pennsylvania, founded by Robert Harper at the turn of the century under the title The Adams Centinel. When his son, Robert G. Harper inherited the editorship, he updated the name. The younger Harper was a conservative Whig, and his paper followed suit. In the middle of the nineteenth century, agriculture was the primary industry in Adams County (pop. 25, 981) but manufacturing in the way of mills, irons forges, and lumbering was also present.

column3: Adventure Magazine (1910-1971) was a pulp magazine originally created by the Ridgway Company first published by the Butterwick Publishing company; Trumbull White was the first editor. He initiated two principle guidelines: the stories published did not have to take place in faraway locales, but it should be a true story, with specific details. Arthur Hoffman became the editor in 1912, and the magazine reached its height of popularity (more than 300,000 subscribers—including Theodore Roosevelt) during his 15 year editorship. At the time of its twenty-fifth anniversary, Time declared Adventure "the number one pulp."
column4: Patrick Scott Belk, "Adventure Magazine: America’s No. 1 Pulp," The Pulp Magazine Project, accessed July 25, 2016.

column3: Art and Archaelogy (1914-34) was an illustrated monthly magazine published by the Archaeological Institute of America; its first editor was Francis Willey Kelsey. The magazine was launched in an effort to popularize the field, although not all members of the organization were in favor of the strategy, worrying that it would dilute the professionalism of their research. The point became moot during the Great Depression, when the circulation dropped too low for recovery, and the magazine folded.

column3: Current Literature (1888-1925) was a monthly magazine founded and edited by Frederick M. Somers out of New York. Somers sold the magazine in 1891; William George Jordan became the new editor. The magazine, which published fiction and literary reviews quickly out rivaled its competitors of larger pages, superior printing quality, and diversity of material. Current Literature reached a circulation of around 40,000 in the middle of the 1890s. Later, the magazine began publishing more news and commentary than fiction and it changed its name to Current Opinion in 1912.

column3: Daily Alta California (1849-1891) was founded in San Francisco (pop. 30,000 in 1856) by Edward C. Kemble. It initially appeared three times a week until 1850, when it became the city’s first daily paper. The populist and independent paper featured the first steam press in the city, which accelerated printing time. The Alta California relayed all state governmental proceedings to the people, making it the newspaper of choice for many locals, even as it faced stiff competition (twelve other newspapers were published in the area in 1854).

column3: Frank Leslie’s Popular Monthly (1876-1956) was a monthly magazine established by New Yorker Frank Leslie. The magazine featured an assortment of material, from poetry to literature reviews to science. When Leslie died in 1880, but his wife continued the publication, even changing her name to his so that there were no discrepancies. The magazine’s circulation surged in circulation in the mid-1880s to around 125,000, but as the economy declined in the wake of the financial Panic of 1893, circulation dropped to around 62,000. In 1905, the name was changed to the American Magazine; it was discontinued in 1956.

column3: Harper’s Monthly Magazine (1850-current) is a monthly magazine founded by the publishers Harper & Brothers out of New York; it was originally called Harper’s New Monthly Magazine but the New was dropped in 1900. Henry J. Raymond was the founding editor, and Henry Mills Alden became editor in the 1890s. Each edition of Harper’s New Monthly Magazine included a variety of topics and genres, from English serials to science features and travel articles. When Scribner’s Monthly came on the scene in 1870, it spurred Harper’s to improve its illustrations, adding more images to each edition and improving the quality. By the 1890s, Harper’s had reached its peak as an illustrated magazine.
column4: Frank Luther Mott, American Journalism: A History of Newspapers in the United States Through 250 Years: 1690 to 1940 (New York: Macmillan, 1941), accessed June 21, 2016, eBook.

column3: Hutchings’ Illustrated California Magazine (1856-61) was a magazine published by James M. Hutchings, an Englishman who moved to California during the 1849 Gold Rush. After making a fortune in mining and losing it in a bank fiasco, Hutchings found success in journalism. His magazine reached a circulation of 8,000, outrivaling the Pioneer by including various local and foreign illustrated pieces. Hutchings was an early booster of Yosemite; he owned a hotel in the area and published the first illustrated magazine promoting the region as a destination for leisure.

column3: Littell’s Living Age (1844-96) was a weekly magazine founded in Boston by Eliakim Littell (succeeded by his son, Robert). The magazine featured literary fiction, particularly from England, and material reprinted from American periodicals and newspapers. The magazine’s circulation remained moderate (under 10,000) until 1880, but production costs were kept low by drawing on previously published material.

column3: Overland Monthly (1868-75; 1883-1923) was a monthly magazine out of San Francisco founded by Anton Roman, a bookseller and publisher. Overland Monthly promoted California and California writers, but also featured famous contributors such as Mark Twain, Bret Harte, Ambrose Bierce, Willa Cather, and Jack London. Similar in style to eastern publications like the Atlantic Monthly and Lippincott’s, Overland was one of the few Californian publications to attract readers in the east. The magazine went through many owners and editors, including writer Bret Harte, who became the editor, and brought the magazine to its first spike in popularity. It had a circulation of 3,000 in the first six months of his editorship. Once Hart left in 1870, however, the magazine dwindled until ceasing publication in 1875. Soon after, Anton Roman started another magazine that was eventually renamed the Overland. This magazine was even more popular than the first, reaching a circulation of 18,000 in 1888, 30,000 by 1900, and 75,000 in 1912. In 1923, the Overland absorbed Out West Magazine and tacked that title onto the end of its name. The Overland Monthly and Out West Magazine was moved to Los Angeles in 1931 before it folded completely in 1935.
column4: Frank Luther Mott, A History of American Magazines 1741-1850 (Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1958), accessed 13 March 2016, eBook.

column3: Pacific Discovery (1948-200?) was a magazine published by the California Academy of Sciences out of San Francisco; Don Greame Kelley was the founding editor. The magazine was originally published as a bi-monthly but switched to a quarterly in 1984. Pacific Discovery focused on natural history and conservation. In 1997, the name was changed to California Wild. Today, the California Academy of Sciences functions primarily as a science education center, housing an aquarium, planetarium, and natural history museum, but it also encompasses the Institute for Biodiversity Science and Sustainability, which employs more than 100 scientists.

column3: Pacific Monthly (1898-1912) was a monthly magazine originally managed by William Brittle Wells out of Portland, Oregan. Wells was a product of the new Stanford University, and was very Western-minded. Therefore, the magazine was founded to promote the West as a destination for both tourists and settlers. Pacific Monthly printed articles about regional politics and culture, as well as fiction articles. In the 1910s, Pacific Monthly, whose area of coverage centered on the Pacific Nrothwest, merged with the magazine Sunset, whose focus was on California and the Southwest (Sunset had been founded by the Southern Pacific Railroad to attract interest in the region.) When the periodicals merged, the new title became Sunset: The Pacific Monthly.

column3: Scribner’s Monthly (1870-1930) was a magazine founded by Dr. Josiah Gilbert Holland, Roswell Smith, and Charles Scribner. This Presbyterian paper quickly came to rival the Methodist Harper’s Monthly, publishing fiction, poems, moral advice, travel guides, current events, and more. Scribner's was especially known for its prominent use of illustrations. In 1885, circulation was about 200,000.

column3: St. John’s Public Ledger (1827-82) was a semi-weekly newspaper founded by Henry Winton out of St. John’s, Newfoundland, Canada. Winton was very outspoken about his anti-catholic beliefs, which led to physical attacks against him that ultimately ended in someone cutting off part of his ears. His son, also Henry Winton, took over the paper in the 1850s. St. John’s was a growing city in the 1870s and had many different small-cale manufacturing industries.

column2: The Dawn: A Journal for Australian Women
column3: The Dawn: A Journal for Australian Women (1888-1905) was a monthly suffragist publication that was founded and published by Louisa Lawson (pen name for Dora Falconer) out of New South Wales, Australia. The newspaper provided a forum for women’s political writing but also published articles on various aspects of women’s lives, including childrearing and cooking. Its primary audience was working class women, and it circulated rurally in rural as well as urban areas.

column3: Touring Topics (1909-current) is a magazine founded by the Auto Club of Southern California and is distributed to its members. The magazine originally focused on automobiles exclusively, and it called for the improvement of local roads and road signs. When Phil Townsend Hanna became the editor of Touring Topics in 1926, the magazine expanded its coverage into the arts, featuring literary pieces and landscapes by local artists, and also travel and tourism, promoting the West Coast as superior to the East Coast for living and for business. In 1934, the publication’s name was changed to Westways to reflect the magazine’s new focus.

column3: The Adams County Free Press (1883-present) is a weekly paper published in Corning, the county seat of Adams County, Iowa. E.C. Russell, a lawyer, founded and edited the paper until his nomination by President Grover Cleveland to port appraiser of Portland, Oregon. J.W. Weed, a saddler, purchased the paper in 1890. R. G. Weissall became the editor in 1900 and continued until 1918. Adams County had a population of 13,601 in 1900, but it declined to 10,998 by 1910. Agriculture was the primary industry in this area.

Kernodle, Sr. was the publisher of the Alamance Gleaner from 1880 to 1944. Graham, which is located in northern North Carolina (pop. 2,366 in 1920), was becoming a manufacturing community in the 1920s, with textile milles, coffin making, and various building material industries.

column3: The Albany Evening Journal (1830-1925) was a daily paper originally founded as an Anti-Mason publication by Thurlow Weed. Under his editorship for 33 years, the paper transitioned from Anti-Masonic to Whig to Republican, becoming the second leading party paper in the state, behind the New York Tribune. Weed is credited with putting the only two Whig presidents in the White House. Interests in politics expanded during the years of the Evening Journal’s publication, and the city’s population increased dramatically with an influx of immigrants. The population reached 50,763 in 1850 (up 50% from the 1840 census) and 90,758 by 1880. The Journal, very affordable for its type, catered to a daily circulation of 4,500, with weekly numbers reaching 18,000 in 1878.

column3: The Allegany County Republican (1880-?) was a weekly newspaper out of Allegany County, New York, which was officially founded in 1880, although had been under various names and owners since 1830. From 1880 to 1901, Lamonte G. Raymond was the sole editor (and sometimes writer) of the paper, well known for his unconventional methods: he seldom wrote out his articles before printing them, preferring to compose the article right in the printing press. When Lewis C. Rice took over the paper, it was circulating 1,086 papers a week around the county (pop. 41,501). Allegany was mainly manufactural at this time, with flour mills and textile factories, but also supported farming and dairy industries.

column3: The American Anthropologist (1888-current) is the journal of the American Anthropological Association. It was originally a quarterly publication of the Anthropological Association of Washington. In 1902, however, it became the official publication of the newly incorporated American Anthropological Association. For a period beginning in 1896, the American Anthropologist was published monthly; today it is a quarterly academic journal.

column3: The Amsterdam Daily Democrat (1879-86) was founded when William J. Kline turned the weekly newspaper, the Democrat, into a daily paper. In 1886, the new daily paper changed its name again, to the Daily Democrat. At three cents a copy, this Republican paper reached a daily circulation by 1887. It served the city of Amsterdam, New York (pop. 16,000 in 1887) and the surrounding areas along the Mohawk River. During the late nineteenth century, this area of upstate New York was a center for the manufacture of household goods; agriculture was also a central economic activity.

column3: The Archiv für Anthropologie (1866-1943) was a quarterly publication founded by Alexander Ecker and Ludwig Lindenschmidt out of Braunschweig, Germany. Ecker was an anthropologiwst interested in craniology, and Lindenschmidt was a historian. The journal, which published anthropological research and information on museum exhibits was the first of its kind in Germany and immediately preceded the founding of the German Anthropological Association. After the founding of the Association, Archiv für Anthropologie became the its flagship publication until World War II disrupted its publication.

column3: The Argus (1846-1957) was a newspaper founded by William Kerr out of Melbourne, Australia; it was originially a biweekly but later switched to a daily. William Kerr only retained control of the newspaper for two years because of libel suits leveled against hi. After Kerr’s departure, the paper became increasingly conservative. Its biggest comepetitor was the Age, which was much more liberal. Although the Argus had a smaller readership, it was read by the elite of the city, giving it more of an impact. In the 1870s, Melbourne was flourishing from the gold rush, attracting many settlers as well as private enterprise.

column3: The Arkansas City Traveler (1870-current) is a daily newspaper out of Arkansas City, Kansas; the founder of the paper is unclear, with sources listing either a Professor Norton or M. G. Mains as the original proprietor. In 1880, the paper became a member of the Associated Press, receiving news by horse or telegram. Thomas W. Eckert and Richard C. Howard became the owners in 1889. Arkansas City had a population of 8,347 in 1890, although this number often fluctuated as American Indian lands farther west were opened up for white settlement and Arkansas City residents moved westward.

column3: The Atlanta Constitution (1868-2001) was a daily newspaper founded by Carey Wentworth Styles out of Atlanta, Georgia. Evan P. Howell became the main editor in 1876 and continued in this role until 1897. An ex-Confederate soldier, Howell became a proponent of the New South, promoting reconciliation through the paper’s news and editorials. In 1878, he started publishing Joel Chandler Harris’ "Uncle Remus" stories. Henry Grady was the managing editor of the Atlanta Constitution in the 1880s during Howell’s editorship; Grady saw the newspaper, in part, as a tool in to promoting Atlanta to northern investors. The Constitution had a daily circulation of 5,000 in 1881. Atlanta was growing throughout the 1880s, increasing from a population of 21,879 in 1880 to 65,533 in 1890. The main industries were cotton and railroad manufacturing. The Constitution rivaled the Atlanta Journal after the Journal was founded in 1883, but the two newspapers merged in 2001 to become the Atlanta Journal-Constitution.

column3: The Augusta Chonicle (1785-current) is a daily newspaper originally founded by Greenberg Hughes out of Augusta, Georgia, as the Augusta Gazette. The paper underwent many name changes but settled at the current Augusta Chonicle in 1885 (5,000 circulation). Patrick Walsh, a city booster and the editor and owner from 1872-99, used the Chonicle as a city booster to attract northern tourism and business and also to voice his disapproval of African-American lynching. Augusta grew significantly in the last part of the nineteenth century, from a population of 21,891 in 1880 to 39,441 in 1900. The main industry was cotton milling.

column3: The Baltimore American (1799-1986) was a newspaper founded by Alexander Martin out of Baltimore, Maryland; it was originally called the American and Daily Advertiser, but changed names numerous times throughout its run. The paper claimed to trace its lineage to the 1773 Maryland Journal and Baltimore Advertiser, but no evidence has been found to verify this. General Felix Angus was the owner in the early 1900s, when the circulation was around 65,554. The city of Baltimore (pop. 508,957 in 1900) suffered from the Great Baltimore Fire during Angus’ reign. The paper was sold to Frank Munsey in 1920, and again in 1923 to William Randolph Hearst who combined the American with the Baltimore News to form the News-American.

Bangor had a population of 16,857 and was known for its lumbering industry.

column3: The Barcaldine Western Champion (1891-1937) was a weekly newspaper founded by William Campbell, Charles James, and Frederic James originally out of Blackall, New South Wales, Australia; however, the paper was moved to Barcaldine in 1886 because the railroad was newly located in Barcaldine. Barcaldine was a new town in the 1880s, and is considered the birthplace of the modern Australian labor movement because many different worker and union meetings were held there. The Barcaldine Western Champion was a conservative paper, editorializing against strikes. The Champion folded during the Great Depression in the 1930s.

column3: The Bismarck Daily Tribune (1881-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Col. Clement A. Lounsberry out of Bismarck, North Dakota (then Dakota Territory). Before becoming a daily, the paper was published as a tri-weekly paper under the name Bismarck Tribune. As the first newspaper in Dakota Territory, the Tribune served readers in a vast geographic area. The paper generally reprinted news received via telegraph, but Lounsberry was the first to break the story of the Battle of Little Big Horn. He had sent a reporter to follow Custer, and while the reporter was killed, his diary survived, and Lounsberry used this as his source. In 1884, Lounsberry sold the Tribune to Marshall Jewell to pursue political and publishing opportunities. Bismarck was a growing town in the 1880s (pop. 2,186 in 1890) as the railroad arrived, bringing gold and new milling industry. The Bismarck Daily Tribune dropped the "Daily" from its title in 1916.

column3: The Boston Atlas (1832-57) was a daily newspaper founded by John Eastburn as a Whig-party press (the Boston Post was the Democratic paper). The paper struggled to gain readership at its onset, but followed the rise of the Whig party in the 1840s to become one of the most popular party papers in the Northeast. It was the first to introduce next-day election results by a type of pony express, and had the outcome of the votes by the next morning’s paper. Even though the Atlas diversified its coverage­–for example, it carried travel correspondence from the new territories–it still followed the decline of the party, and in 1857, was merged with other small newspapers in Boston to become the Boston Traveller.

column3: The Brooklyn Daily Eagle (1841-1955) was a daily newspaper founded by Isaac Van Anden and Henry Cruse Murphy; it was originally created as a Democratic Party organ for the 1842 election, but continued long after the political contest. Walt Whitman was its most famous editor, serving in this capacity from 1846-48. St. Clair McKelway was the editor from 1886-1915. During the 1890s, the Brooklyn Daily Eagle commanded the highest daily afternoon newspaper circulation in the country. The Eagle was outspoken against the consolidation of Brooklyn with the rest of New York and editorialized against the political machines that ran the city. After more than one hundred years of circulation, the paper folded in 1955 after a prolonged strike of the New York Newspaper Guild. Attempts to revive the paper in the 1960s were unsuccessful. The current Brooklyn paper entitled the Brooklyn Daily Eagle has no technical relation to the historical newspaper.

column3: The Chicago Daily Tribune (1847-current) is a daily newspaper founded by James Kelly and John E. Wheeler out of Chicago. Initially, the paper only had 400 subscribers, even though Chicago itself had a population of 16,000 at that time. James Medill (later mayor of Chicago) and Dr. Charles Ray, editors in the 1850s, greatly increased the Tribune’s circulation. The paper was a strong supporter of the Republican Party, and especially Abraham Lincoln, who often came to conferences at the Tribune offices. By the end of the Civil War, the paper had a circulation of just under 47,000. The Tribune covered local news. scandals, and sports, and it published literary pieces from well known writers, even printing a revised edition of the New Testament in 1881. In the 1880s, with Medill still serving as editor, the paper devoted significant coverage to labor unrest, including the Haymarket Affair; at this time, daily circulation was around 60,000. The 1890’s brought the World’s Fair to Chicago, then the second largest city in the nation (1.2 million pop.). When Medill died in 1899, he left a newspaper legacy behind him that his family took up, especially his grandchildren, who all went into the newspaper business at the Tribune and other newspapers. In 1963, the paper dropped the Daily from its name.

column3: The <i>Christian Advocate and Journal (1833-1865) was a weekly Methodist Episcopal newspaper out of New York, which has had variations of the original title name throughout its publication. Barber Badger was the titular director of the original publication, but the paper fell under the auspices of various editors until 1840, when Thomas E. Bond took over. Bond was a dentist and a minister, and he was chosen as the paper’s editor by the Methodist Conference because of his steadfastness to Church ideals, during a time of religious and political turmoil. The Church was split over the issue of slavery, and the pro-slavery churches split off from the conference in 1845. Despite the controversy, the newspaper retained a circulation of 25,000 that year, with a price of $1.25 per issue.

column3: The Christian Science Monitor (1908-current) is a daily publication out of Boston founded by Mary Baker Eddy, also founder of the First Church of Christ, Scientist. Eddy began the paper to provide an alternative to periodicals known for their yellow journalism and for attacking her church. Though the First Church of Christ, Scientist, owns the Christian Science Monitor, it professes secular reporting. Erwin D. Canham was the editor from 1940-64, and he is credited with increasing circulation and expanding the paper’s scope to worldwide news coverage. The Monitor reached its highest circulation in 1970, with 223,000 readers.

column3: The Cincinnati Enquirer (1841-current) is a daily newspaper founded by John and Charles Brough out of Cincinnati. It boasts the oldest continuous Sunday edition in print in the United States today; while it was not the first newspaper to print regularly on Sundays, the others have been discontinued. Founder John Brough started his newspaper career at the age of twelve, when he apprenticed with papers in his home of Marietta, Ohio. He also cultivated an early interest in politics, becoming state auditor and a member of the Ohio legislature. With his brother Charles, he bought the Cincinnati Advertiser in 1841, and changed its name to the Enquirer. The new paper followed John’s political allegiances, starting off as a Democratic paper, but switching to a National Union Party (Republican) paper around election time in 1864. John was nominated by the National Union Party to become governor, and served from 1864-65, when he died of gangrene from an ankle sprain. However, by this point, the Cincinnati Enquirer was already under the ownership of Washington McLean, who passed down the paper to his son, John, in 1881; John McLean more than quintupled the paper’s circulation to around 90,000. Cincinnati had a population of 300,000 in 1890, with large industries in iron production and meat packing.

column3: The Daily Democratic State Journal (1853-1858) was a morning paper founded in Sacramento, California, by V.E. Geiger and Co. After a crippling fire in the printing office, the paper changed ownership and editorship no less than five times, until it was given up for good. Sacramento itself, newly incorporated as a city in 1850, was devastated throughout the decade by fires, floods, and cholera. Despite this, the population was quickly growing, reaching upwards of 10,000 in the early ’50s. The Daily Democratic State Journal was the only newspaper at the time to feature a foreign language (French) column for this expanding population.

column3: The Daily Evening Bulletin (1855-95) was a daily newspaper founded by James King of William out of San Francisco. The newspaper’s unrelenting attacks on everyone and everything wrong with the city generated a circulation of 3,500, the largest in the city, within two months of being in print. The bold reporting ultimately led to the founder’s death, however, when King published an article about James Casey, an ex-convict turned city supervisor, and Casey fatally shot King in response. The paper continued to flourish long after King’s death, however, benefitting from San Francisco’s rapid growth during the gold rush. The city had a population of 56, 802 in 1860.

column3: The Daily Morning News (1850-64) was a newspaper founded by William Tappan Thompson out of Savannah, Georgia. Although the paper declared itself neutral in its political associations, Thompson was clearly pro-southern, so much so that he had to flee when Sherman’s army came through the area. The two cent Daily Morning News started by reporting local news but also increasingly covered state and national issues, too. When the paper started, Savannah had a population of 15,000. The 1850s were a time of expansion in the city, with the ports and railroads active with shipping and trading enterprises.

column3: The Daily Picayune (1837-1914) was a daily newspaper founded by Colonel Alva Morris Holbrook, George Wilkins Kendall, and Frances Asbury Lumsden out of New Orleans. When Holbrooke died in 1876, his wife, Eliza Jane, took over the failing paper and brought it to success. Soon after, she married George Nicholson, and they then ran the paper together. The Picayune covered a wide variety of topics, from sports to politics to society, but catered primarily to an upper class audience. The paper had a circulation of 10,000 in 1881, and the city had a growing population at this time, from 216,090 in 1880 to 242,039 in 1890. The paper was consolidated with the Times in 1914, and currently goes by the name Times-Picayune.

column3: The Daily Placer Times and Transcript (1853-54) was a Democratic newspaper founded by G. K. Fitch and L. Pickering, originally out of Sacramento, California, but soon after removed to San Francisco. The paper was formed after the merging of two rival Sacramento newspapers: Placer Times and Transcript. There is a discrepancy in the historical record as to why the paper switched cities. Some sources say that the consolidated paper moved after another rival, The State Journal, threatened their circulation, while others say that the paper was so successful from its merging that it moved to San Francisco to gain a wider readership. Newly incorporated as a city in 1850, San Francisco grew with the gold rush throughout the decade to reach a population of 56, 802 in 1860.

column3: The Fayetteville Observer (1833-65, 1883-present) is a weekly paper originally founded as the Carolina Observer by Francis Waldo in Fayetteville, North Carolina. In 1824, Waldo sold it to E. The newspaper’s offices were burned by Sherman’s army in 1865, but the paper was revived by Hale’s sons in 1883.

column3: The Fedco Reporter (?-1999) was the monthly magazine of the Federal Distribution Company out of Southern California. Fedco was a members-only wholesale store originally established by 800 post office workers who wanted a means to obtain necessities at a lower cost. John Bregoli was the editor of the Fedco Reporter in 1996, and most articles were written by freelancers. The Fedco Reporter had a circulation of 2 million during the mid 1990s. The store, and therefore the magazine, went out of business in 1999, when Fedco, which only operated in Southern California, was no longer able to compete with national grocery and wholesale chains.

column3: The Fresno Republican (1877-1932) was a weekly newspaper founded by Dr. Chester A. Rowell, out of Fresno, California. Modeled after the Springfield Republican in Massachusetts, it sought to generate Republican Party loyalty in California and also to help enforce law and instill peace. Rowell was appalled at the state of lawlessness he encountered when he first arrived in Fresno from Massachusetts. Rowell’s Republican didn’t do well, however, and he sold the failing paper to S. Adison Miller in 1879. Miller turned the paper around and by 1885, it had reached a circulation of 1,128 in 1885. Later, Rowell’s nephew Chester H. became the editor of the Fresno Republican and he took up his uncle’s crusade against the lawlessness, specifically targeting alcohol, sex, and gambling industries, especially in Chinatown. Fresno flourished thanks to the railroad and became a center of agriculture, especially grapes. In 1876, it had a population of over 3,000.

column3: The Friend (1827-1955) was a magazine that was founded by the Elias Hicks faction of the Society of Friends, under the editorship of Robert Smith. It was read in the west in addition to the Quaker stronghold of Philadelphia and its surrounding areas. While the goal was to foster Quaker faith, The Friend published a wide variety of content including science and travel articles, poetry, current news, and anti-war and pro-temperance articles. It was joined with the Friends Intelligencer in 1955 to form the Friends Journal, symbolizing the reunification of the two different Quaker factions who split because of differences in religious practice, among other concerns.

column3: The Gazette (1778-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Fleury Mesplet out of Montreal, Quebec. The paper was Benjamin Franklin’s idea, designed incite the city to join the colonies in fighting for independence from Britain. Because Mesplet was delayed by travel and incarceration, however, the Gazette’s message was late, and the paper was not launched until 1778. The Gazette was originally printed entirely in French, but soon added English. In 1822, the paper switched to English only. In 1870, Thomas and Richard White became both the editors and owners. Montreal, the busiest port in Canada in the 1800s, experienced a population boom at the beginning of the 1880s. Major industries included textiles and shoe making.

column3: The Irish Times (1859-current) is a daily and weekly newspaper founded by Major Lawrence Knox out of Dublin, Ireland, United Kingdom. He was a conservative Protestant who supported the unionization of Ireland and Great Britain, and his paper reflected those beliefs. The Irish Times routinely reprinted articles from American newspapers, such as the New York World. Sir John Arnott owned the paper in the 1890s, which had a circulation of approximately 28,500. The Irish Times main competitor was the Freeman’s Journal which had a greater readership because it circulated outside of Dublin.

column3: The Kansas City Star (1880-current) is a daily newspaper founded by William Rockhill Nelson and Samuel E. Morss out of Kansas City, Missouri. At two cents an issue (compared to the five cent price of its competitors), the paper was able to command the greatest circulation in the city within three months of its founding. By 1887, it had a circulation of 25,000 a day (city pop.132,716 in 1890). The Kansas City Star’s primary focus was local news, including calling for a citywide system of parks and paved streets, which were created thanks in part to the paper’s advocacy. Ernest Hemingway wrote for the Star in 1917-18 and credited his editor with improving his writing style.

column3: The Lake Wakatip Mail (1863-1947) was a weekly newspaper out of Queenstown, New Zealand; Englishman William Warren became the sole proprietor of the Mail in 1867, and his family ran the paper through the First World War. Today, the town is a popular destination for adventure tourism.

column3: The Leavenworth Times (1857-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Robert Crozier out of Leavenworth, Kansas. It was originally a weekly Republican, and Colonel Daniel Read Anthony (brother to social reformer and suffragist Susan B. Anthony) reinforced the paper’s political leanings when he bought the Leavenworth Times and expanded it into a daily in 1871. Anthony was so outspoken in the newspaper’s editorial pages that he provoked anger from political opponents, even exchanging gunfire with them in the streets of Leavenworth. The Anthony family continued to run the paper until the 1960s. Leavenworth (pop. ­­­­20,735 in 1900) is home to the military base Fort Leavenworth, which has been active in Northeast Kansas since 1827.

column3: The Los Angeles Star (1851-75) was a weekly newspaper founded by John A. Lewis and E. Gould in Los Angeles, California. Initially, the paper gained circulation because it featured news in Spanish as well as English. The paper had no official political position at its founding started, but in 1856, when Henry Hamilton, an Irishman, was named editor, it became a Democratic paper. By this point, the Los Angeles Star had dropped its Spanish pages, but it still outperformed the other two papers in town, the Southern Californian and El Clamor Pùblico. The paper printed national news when it was available, in addition to local and literary pieces.

column3: The Los Angeles Times (1881-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Nathan Cole, Jr. and Thomas Gardiner out of Los Angeles, California. It was originally the Daily Times, but dropped the Daily from its title in 1886. Harrison Gray Otis became publisher of the paper in 1882. The Times has printed various supplements over the course of its lifespan, including the Los Angeles Times Magazine. Los Angeles was growing throughout the 1880s due to the railroad and advertisements about the city’s favorable climate; it reached a population of 50,395 in 1890. Otis’s son-in-law, Henry Chandler, took over the paper in 1917. The city reached a population of one million in 1924. Henry’s son, Norman, took over the Los Angeles Times in 1944. In the first part of the twenty-first century, the Los Angeles Times has the fourth largest newspaper circulation in the country, after USA Today, the Wall Street Journal and the New York Times.

column3: The Lowell Daily Citizen and News (1856-94) was a daily newspaper founded by Leonard Brown and George F. Murray, out of Lowell, Massachusetts. It was a consolidation of three different papers: the Daily Morning Paper, the American Citizen and the Daily Citizen. Chauncey Knapp became the editor in 1859, and the paper continued under him through the 1880s. A staunch anti-slavery supporter, he brought the paper to a circulation of 1,000 daily in 1878. Lowell was a prominent industrial center during this time.

column3: The Maitland Mercury and Hunter River General Advertiser (1843-present) is a newspaper (originally a weekly) founded by Richard Jones and Thomas William Tucker out of Maitland, New South Wales, Australia. The partnership between Tucker and Jones dissolved soon after the paper’s founding, but Tucker continued to run the Mercury independently. By 1893, the Hunter General Advertiser portion of the name had been dropped. The paper was successful because of its broad coverage spannin local, national, and international news. Maitland, located on the Hunter River in the Hunter Valley region of New South Wales, was a center of mining and industry in the last half of the nineteenth century, and is also known for the Maitland Gaol, which was the longest running gaol in Australia from 1844-1998.

column3: The Messenger (1887-1908) was a newspaper founded by Julius A. Bonitz out of Wilmington, North Carolina; it had weekly, semi-weekly, and daily editions. Thomas Clawson was the editor of the Semi-Weekly Messenger in 1898, and he is responsible for playing an essential role in provoking the race riot that occurred in Wilmington in that year, as he printed pro-lynching and white supremacist articles. Wilmington, in southeast North Carolina, had a population of 20,976 in 1900.

column3: The Morning Press (1863-1937) was a daily Republican newspaper founded by Reverend J. A. Johnson out of Santa Barbara, California; it was originally called the Post, but Johnson changed the name to the Press in 1869. John T. Johnston and George Knepper took over the paper in 1892. The Morning Press had a circulation of 1,080 in 1893. Santa Barbara was suffering from the financial Panic of 1893 and anxiously waiting for the railroad to connect them to San Francisco, which would eventually increas tourism and lead to population growth. This did not happen until 1901.

column3: The New Hampshire Sentinel (1799-current) is a weekly newspaper founded by John Prentiss out of Keene, New Hampshire. Prentiss was 21 when he started the paper, which had just 70 subscribers at the onset. He accepted various forms of household goods as payment. The paper switched owners multiple times after Prentiss’ retirement in 1849 until his grandson William H. Prentiss purchased the paper in 1880. During its earliest years, the New Hampshire Sentinel was identified first as a Federalist, then as a Whig and Republican paper. The Sentinel had a circulation of 3,800 in 1878. Keene (pop. of 6,786 in 1880) had prominent chair making and milling industries.

column3: The New York Evening Express (1836-81) was a Whig newspaper that was founded in 1836 by brothers James and Erastus Brooks. It was noted for its hotel lists (lists of guests staying in local hotels) and political news, and especially its impossibly fast election results. Once, the Express went so far as to hire a boat which it outfitted with a press so that by the time it travelled from the state capital in Albany, where the results were announced, to New York City, newspapers with the election returns were ready for sale. Circulation of penny press papers like the Express increased in New York City, during mid-century, reaching around one paper for every five citizens in 1850. The paper switched political alliances in the early 1850s from the Whig to the Know-Nothing Party, but switched again to a Democratic paper as the former party dissolved. After the brothers’ deaths, the paper was sold and merged with another paper in 1881, to become the Evening Mail and Express.

column3: The New York Herald (1835-1924) is a daily newspaper founded by James Gordon Bennett out of New York City. Bennett was a revolutionary in the newspaper business, as he founded one of the first penny papers, and changed the way local, foreign, and economic news were reported. His son of the same name took over in 1866, and was just as unconventional as his father. However, Bennett Jr. was much more frivolous with his spending, which eventually contributed to the paper failing. The paper commanded a circulation of around 150,000 in the 1870s, and reached its apex in 1885, with a circulation of 190,500.

column3: The New York Press (1887-1916) was a daily newspaper out of New York City. Henry L. Einstein was the owner from 1895 until Frank Munsey bought the paper in 1912; Munsey wanted a morning daily to support Theodore Roosevelt in the 1912 election. Erwin Wardman was the editor in the late 1890s, and he is credited with first printing the phrase "yellow journalism" to describe the sensational news reporting of rival papers, the New York Journal and the New York World. Munsey folded the New York Press into the New York Sun in 1916.

column3: The New York Recorder (1891-96) was a daily newspaper out of New York City; George Turner was the original proprieter and Eliza Putnam Heaton was the editor of the women’s page, for which it was well known. In 1893, the Recorder became the first American newspaper to publish advertisements and comics in color; however other newspapers, followed soon after. The paper was folded into the New York Tribune in 1896.

column3: The New York Times (1851-present) was originally a daily Whig newspaper founded in New York City by Henry J. Raymond (editor), George Jones, and Edward B. Wesley. They saw an opening for a penny paper covering foreign and local news, but after a year had to raise the price to two cents to cover their expenses. The paper’s circulation was around 26,000 at the time of the increase, dipped after it, but soon recovered. As the Republican Party came into prominence in the mid 1850s, the paper switched allegiances. The paper kept up and improved its circulation through the 1870s, but started on a hard and fast decline after, until Adolph Ochs took over the paper in 1893. The paper at this time had a circulation of 9,000 and was losing money, but Ochs brought it out of its slump, steadily increasing circulation through the next 25 years of his management.
column4: Frank Luther Mott, American Journalism: A History of Newspapers in the United States Through 250 Years: 1690 to 1940 (New York: Macmillan, 1941), 278-81, 428-29, 549-51.

column3: The New-Bedford Mercury (1807-95), out of the whaling town of New Bedford, Massachusetts, was a weekly paper founded by Benjamin Lindsey. It remained under Lindsay until his son, also Benjamin Lindsay, took over in 1831. Lindsay, Jr. added a daily paper in addition to the weekly to increase circulation, which was successful. The Mercury had no competition during this time and kept current subscribers by informing them of all the comings and goings of the ships in the area. Newly incorporated as a city in 1847, New Bedford was prosperous with a population of 16,000. Whaling was the main industry here, and brought great success economically. The paper originally had Federalist tendencies, but then, at various times, leaned toward the Whig and Republican parties.

column3: The Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners’ Advocate (1876-current) is a daily newspaper out of Newcastle, New South Wales, Australia. It was founded by James Fletcher (proprietor), John Miller Sweet (manager who had founded the Miners’ Advocate earlier in 1876), and Thomas Corrigan (editor). This liberal and independent paper published news important to miners in the area, and also shipping news involving the harbor, because these were the two main industries in the 1870s in Newcastle. In 1980, the Miners’ Advocate was dropped from the title.

column3: The Newfoundlander (1827-1884) was a semi-weekly newspaper founded by John Shea, out of St. John’s, Newfoundland, Canada. The paper passed from Shea brother to Shea brother (John, William, Ambrose) until the youngest brother, Edward Dalton, acquired it in 1846. A politician, the paper followed his political interests from Liberal and Catholic views to crossing parties to support the Confederacy movement. St. John’s was known throughout the nineteenth century for its prominence as a fishery hub.

column3: The North Atago Times (1864-1932) was a weekly newspaper founded by Frank Pinkerton out of Oamaru, North Atago, on New Zealand’s South Island; it was originally called Oamaru Times and Waitaki. When William Jukes Stewardson bought the paper in 1870, he changed the name and also began publishing the paper daily. In the 1870s, Oamaru was expanding its ports and railroads to better facilitate shipping enterprises. The town was also known for its limestone production and limestone buildings. The Times ceased publication in 1932 as a recult of competition with the Oamaru Mail.

The Herald only had 53 subscribers at first, but by 1866, it commanded the largest circulation in the state. Omaha had a population of 30,518 in 1880 and boasted a wide variety of different manufacturing enterprises, including breweries and iron works.

column3: The Pacific Coast Archaeological Society Quarterly (1965-current) is a journal published by the Pacific Coast Archaeological Society out of Orange County, California. Later, the quarterly’s focus shifted to the history and prehistory of the area and its original inhabitants. The journal publishes the work of both professionals and lay people interested with in the field. Keith Dixon, Beth Padon, and Jane Rosenthal were the editors in 1992.

The siblings continued to control the paper through the early 1900s, with Allen running the editorial side, and Robert providing financing through his career as a circus manager. Agriculture was the main industry for Perry (pop. 4,630 in 1910) at the beginning of the twentieth century.

column3: The Pioneer (1877-98) was a weekly newspaper founded by Alex P. Murgotten out of San Jose, California. Murgotten was a member of the Pioneer Society of Santa Clara County, which had called for each pioneer member to share his pioneer story. Murgotten thought that the newspaper would be an efficient way to do this while also providing a means to communicate other information relevant to the Pioneers, such as local county news. The paper had a circulation of fewer than 1,000 in 1880. San Jose was primarily agricultural at this time.

column3: The Pioneer is a monthly children's magazine that released its first issue on March 15, 1924. During the Soviet era, this Moscow-based publication drew its origins from the concurrent Pioneer Youth movement initiated by Lenin. Primarily serving as a source of communist teachings for schoolchildren age 9-15, this publication featured a variety of stories and reached a circulation of 1.5 million in 1975. The magazine continues to be published today, but with a much smaller circulation (around 1,500).

column3: The Pittsburg Press (1887-1992) was a daily newspaper founded by Thomas Johnston Keenan Jr. out of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. The newspaper referred to itself and the city of Pittsburgh without the "h" until 1927. John S. Ritenour was the first editor of the Press, which was sold to Oliver S. Hershman in 1901. Both Keenan and Hershman were major sports fans, and the paper reflected this interest with large spreads on baseball, cycling, and boxing. Pittsburgh (pop. 321,616 in 1900) was an industrial city during the early twentieth century, and was especially known for its steel. The Press was combined with the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette in 1992, and an online version was created in 2011.

Liberty Holden bought the newspaper in 1885 and hired Elbert Baker and Charles Kennedy to run it. Under Holden’s ownership, the Plain Dealer moved away from its previous Democratic. Cleveland (pop. 381,768) was an industrial city during the early twentieth century, with high oil and steel production.

column3: The Public Ledger (1836-1925) was a daily newspaper out of Philadelphia. Organized by William Swain and Arunah Abell, it was one of the first penny papers in the city, and it launched as an experiment with plans to shut down after one year if it proved unsuccessful. After just eight months, however, the paper was commanded a circulation of 10,000, and it grew to 20,000 less than a year later, outcompeting by thousands the dailies in the city. The politically neutral Ledger printed all sorts of national and local news, with everything from temperance to reprintings of poetry. In 1847, albeit under much skepticism, the Ledger acquired the first ever rotary printer, speeding up its press time and starting a new era in newspaper printing.

column3: The Queensland Times (1859-current) is a newspaper originally founded out of Ipswich, Queensland, Australia, as the Ipswich Herald; the first editor of this weekly was J.C. Thompson. The paper originated as a means to advocate for the separation of present-day Queensland from New South Wales; however, the separation was formalized right before the first edition of the Ipswich Herald was printed. In 1861, Hugh Parkinson bought the paper, and changed its name to its current title. The Queensland Times reported primarily local and regional news. It became a daily paper in 1908. Ipswich was growing in the last half of the nineteenth century because of its connections to the railroad.

column3: The Reading Times and Dispatch (1870-1914) was a daily newspaper founded by Jacob Knabb out of Reading, Pennsylvania. He owned both the Evening Dispatch and the Reading Times, which he combined in 1870, dropping the Evening out of the Dispatch, to make the new paper. Thomas C. Zimmerman was the editor, and the paper had a circulation of 3,000 in 1878. In 1881, the paper shortened its title to just the Reading Times. Reading was an industrial hub, with the Reading Railroad connecting the town to Philadelphia and from there, the rest of the country.

column3: The Rocky Mountain News (1859-2009) was a daily newspaper out of Denver, Colorado, founded by William Byers. The paper began circulating in the midst of Denver’s goldrush, and Byers was a city booster. The Rocky Mountain News had a circulation of 2,000 in 1878. In the 1880s, the population was 35,629, but growing with the local silver industry. By 1890, the News faced competition for circulation from five different newspapers, but continued to be profitable. The paper did not fold until 2009 when the recession–and internet news sources–took a toll on circulation numbers, ultimately closing down the oldest newspaper in the state.

column3: The San Diego Union (1868-1992) was a daily newspaper founded by William Jeff Gatewood out of San Diego, California. At its onset, circulation spanned Southern California, Arizona territory, and parts of northern Mexico. John D. Spreckles took over the paper in 1890;, and he increased circulation to around 4,000 by 1898, despite the economic downturn accompanying the Panic of 1893. In 1928, the Copley family bought the San Diego Union, and ran it until 2009. James Copley was in charge of the paper in the 1960s, when the city experienced another economic downturn. In 2009, the San Diego Union was combined with the San Diego Evening Tribune to form the Union-Tribune, which continues to be published today.

column3: The San Francisco Chronicle (1865-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Charles and Michael de Young out of San Francisco and originally named the Dramatic Chronicle; in 1869, the drama section of the paper split off into the Daily Dramatic Review, leaving just the main San Francisco Chronicle. This paper grew to 6,000 subscribers in its first year, and to 39,150 subscribers in its first decade. By 1867, it had dropped all political associations. In the early 1890s, the San Francisco Chronicle publishers held a counterpart to the Chicago’s World’s Fair, an event that attracted more than a million people to San Francisco, a city whose population was only 300,000; this increased the newspaper’s visibility. The San Francisco earthquake and fire of 1906 was a significant tragedy for the city, and it spurred the three main San Francisco papers (the Chronicle, Examiner, and Morning Call) to issue an emergency issue together. By 1961, the paper reached a circulation of 300,000 which was about half of the city’s population (740,316), even though it was still competing with the Examiner.

column3: The San Francisco Daily Herald (1850-56) was a Democratic newspaper founded by John Nugent in San Francisco. It was the most popular paper in the city until it printed a pro-Southern article, and its rival, the Alta, got the edge. San Francisco’s population was 30,000 in 1856, but quickly gaining with the gold rush.

column3: The San Francisco Examiner (1865-current) is a daily newspaper out of San Francisco, California. It was founded in 1863 as the Democratic Press and as a Civil War paper, it was a staunch supporter of the Confederate cause. After Lincoln’s assassination, protesters destroyed its offices. The paper was restarted as the Daily Examiner in 1865. George Hearst acquired the paper in 1880, but it was running at a loss until his son, William Randolph, took over in 1887. The junior Hearst’s goal was to acquire a large readership­–including members of the working class–over which he could exert political influence, in part through the technique of yellow journalism. The San Francisco had a population of 298,997 in 1890 and Examiner was the beginning of the Hearst media empire.

column3: The Santa Barbara Daily Press (1872-1885) is a daily newspaper founded by Joseph Johnson out of Santa Barbara, California. He started the paper with the goals of sparking tourism in the area, promoting the white majority, and transforming the ranches into agricultural areas. Johnson had a fierce rivalry with Santa Barbara Times’ editor Jarrett Richards, especially when it came to the proposed subsidies that would bring the railroad to town. These tensions eventually escalated into a full-blown fistfight, which Johnson lost. General Otis took over the Santa Barbara Daily Press from 1876 to 1880, when Robert D. Bogart came into possession of it. Santa Barbara was increasingly becoming known as a tourist destination by this time.

column3: The Scientific American (1845-current) is a monthly magazine founded by Rufus Porter out of New York. In addition to articles about science, the first few issues included poetry and fiction. The publication of literary material was discontinued when the paper was sold to Orson Desaix Munn and Alfred Ely Beach. The paper flourished under Munn & Company, who brought the circulation up to 50,000 in the late 1880s. The paper attracted many different inventors and scientists, such as Samuel J. Morse and Thomas Edison; they were especially known for their role in setting up a patent office for their inventors. The Scientific American is the longest running magazine in the United States.

column3: The South Australian Register (1836-1931) was a newspaper founded by Robert Thomas and George Stevenson out of Adelaide, South Australia, Australia. The first issues were actually printed in London as a means of promoting the colony, before South Australia was officially founded, as a promotion for the colony. These initial issues were sporadically printed, but the South Australian Register became a regular daily in the 1850s. The outspokenness of its early editors provoked a number of libel suits against the paper. The Register primarily reported on local news, but it printed stories from all over the world, as its offices had a telegraph connection to London in the 1870s. Robert Kyffin Thomas was the editor from 1878 to 1910, when the Register’s main competitor was the South Australian Advertiser. Circulation was around 6,000 in 1884, and the region experienced a wheat boom in the 1870s and 1880s.

column3: The Spirit of the Times (1819-1856) was a weekly newspaper out of Batavia, New York, founded by Oran Follett. In 1845, after changing hands multiple times, the Times came under the jurisdiction of William Seaver and his son. William Seaver is perhaps best known for publishing, with his brother, James, the story of Mary Jemison, a white settler who was taken captive by Seneca Indians and who lived with them for the majority of her life. James recorded Jemison’s story in 1823, although clearly with his own interpretation of the material, and published the first editions of her captivity narrative. After his death, William reworked the successful story and reprinted it from his own publishing house; by this time, Batavia was becoming more connected to the rest of the state via railroad and telegraph lines, and the story gained wider circulation. By 1850, Batavia had a population of just over 2,000 people, with multiple small and home businesses.

column3: The Springfield Daily Republican (1844-1946) was a daily newspaper originally founded as the weekly Republican by Samuel Bowles (1824) out of Springfield, Massachusetts. His son by the same name founded the daily version, although he had no subscribers at the onset. Launching a daily paper was risky because at the time, there were few in the state, outside of Boston. However, the paper turned into a long-running success. Bowles was friends with Emily Dickinson, who printed some of her work in the paper. The Republican’s name is misgiving as it was founded before the Republican Party began. Originally, the paper had Whig affiliations, but once Republican Party was founded and rose to prominence, the paper briefly followed it before dropping all political affiliations and becaming neutral. Springfield, Massachusetts had a population of 14,000 in 1850, the year it became incorporated into a city. It was known for its paper manufacturing.

column3: The St. Louis Post-Dispatch (1879-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Joseph Pulitzer out of St. Louis, Missouri. It is a merger of the two papers, the former St. Louis Dispatch (founded 1863) and St. Louis Post (founded 1875). During the early years of Pulitzer’s editorship at the Post-Dispatch, Pulitzer’s focus was on accurate reporting and the exposure of corruption. The Post-Dispatch had a healthy circulation of 22,000 in 1881. Later, however, Pulitzer’s newspaper, and especially the New York World, would become known for their yellow journalism, sensational resporting designed to increase readership, especialy in competition against rival papers. St. Louis was a growing industrial city at the time with a population of 451,770 in 1890.

column3: The Steuben Farmers’ Advocate (1816-1938) was a weekly newspaper founded by Captain Benjamin Smead out of Bath, Steuben County, New York; it was originally named Steuben and Allegany Patriot. In 1822, the paper became the Farmer’s Advocate and Steuben Advertiser, and then adopted to its final name when it was sold to Perry S. Donahe in 1857. By 1860, Donahe had already sold the paper again, this time to Anthony L. Underhill, who remained connected to the paper for the next forty years. Underhill was a booster for the Democratic Party, and the paper followed suit. During Underhill’s reign, the paper was innovative in its printing techniques, becoming the first local paper to use the cylinder press, and the first to use steam power for both printing and folding. In 1880, the population of Bath was 7,396, and the main industries were agriculture and lumbering.

column3: The Telegraph and Messenger (1869-85) was a weekly newspaper out of Macon, Georgia, originally edited by Joseph Clisby. It was formed by merging the Macon Telegraph (founded 1926) and the Journal and Messenger (founding date unclear). Striving for neutrality in its columns, the Republican Telegraph and Messenger stood directly opposed to the Democratic Atlanta Constitution. The Telegraph and Messenger had a weekly circulation of 1,728 in 1878. In the 1880s, Harry Stillwell Edwards took over the editorship and John Fletcher Hanson took over the ownership. Together they called for technical college in Georgia, which eventually led to the founding of the Georgia Institute of Technology. Macon had a population of 12,749 in 1880, and textile mills and agriculture (particularly peaches) were the main industries.

column3: The Times-Tribune (1903-current) is a newspaper originally out of Alexandria, Indiana, a town just north of Indianapolis. The newspaper was formed when the Times Company combined the Times (founded in 1885) and the Tribune (founded in 1898) to form the new paper, both of which were founded by Thomas French. French was instrumental in encouraging natural gas speculation in the area, which which ultimately drew manufacturors, including glassmakers, to set up business in the area. A population boom followed as the city grew from from 715 in 1890 to 7,221 in 1900. Today, the Times-Tribune is a weekly published out of Elwood, Indiana, ten miles west of Alexandria.

column3: The Times (1785-current) is a daily newspaper out of London, England, United Kingdom, founded by John Walter; it was originally called the The Daily Universal Register, but the paper changed names in 1788. The Times had remarkable editors throughout the 1800s: from Thomas Barnes (1817-41), who brought the independent paper into the public eye, to John T. Delane, who changed the way newspapers cover war by creating a war correspondent position. The circulation of the paper increased during the first half of the nineteenth century to 40,000 in 1850, but the last half of the century produced a decline in circulation. Today, the London Times is considered a newspaper of record.

column3: The Utica Daily Observer (1848-84) was a daily paper founded by Eliasaph Dorchester out of Utica, New York. The paper originated as the weekly Observer, but the daily was created in 1848. Dewitt C. Grove became the editor in 1852 when the Observer combined with the Democrat, and he held that position for the next thirty years. During this time, he also took on E. Prentiss Bailey as a partner, and Bailey succeeded him as editor. During the next 75 years, Baileys would work for the Utica Daily Observer. The paper had a circulation of 3,500 in 1876; the city was known as a trade hub.

column3: The Wagga Wagga Advertiser or Daily Advertiser (1868-current) is a newspaper founded by Thomas Darlow and Auber George Jones, both rich pastoralists, out of Wagga Wagga, New South Wales, Australia. The paper was originially a biweekly but progressed to a daily in 1910. The paper was founded to promote Wagga Wagga, provide local agricultural news, and to inform the public about broader world news in general, which was provided via telegraph. Another appeal of the Advertiser was the high quality paper it was printed on. The railway reached Wagga Wagga in 1879, which allowed the town to expand.

The Warwick area, and founder George Clark, were known for prominence in wool production.

column3: The Washington Post (1877-current) is a daily newspaper founded by Stilson Hutchins out of Washington, D. C.; it was originally used to promote the Democratic Party, but it had dropped political associations by 1888. Frank Hatton and Beriah Wilkins bought the Washington Post in 1889, increasing the reputation and circulation of the paper. They hired John Philips Sousa to write the "Washington Post March" for an essay contest that they sponsored The Washington Post had a circulation of about 16,000 every day and 20,000 weekly during the l890s. In 1905, the paper was sold to John R. McLean, who printed many sensational stories. Today, the Washington Post is one of the nation’s leading newspapers and has won over 40 Pulitzer Prizes.

column3: The World (1860-1931) was a daily newspaper founded by Alexander Cummings out of New York. Originally a religious broadside, it switched to Republican affiliations and then to Democratic as it changed owners. Once Joseph Pulitzer acquired the paper, all associations were dropped and the World became entangled in the now famous rivalry with William Randolph Hearst’s New York Journal, ushering in the era of Yellow Journalism. Both papers went to extraordinary lengths to encourage and then cover the Spanish-American War, chartering boats to send reporters to Cuba to cover the story. Though this brought the World’s circulation to 1.5 million, the paper was running a deficit and had to scale back. In 1931, the World merged with the New York Evening Telegram to form the World-Telegram.

column3: The Youth’s Companion (1827-1929) was a weekly magazine founded by Nathaniel Willis out of Boston. The periodical originally had a narrowly religious focus, but once Daniel Sharp Ford took over in 1857, the magazine expanded its content to include various materials and short story serials written by popular writers including Harriet Beecher Stowe, Mark Twain, and Jack London. The changed focus was reflected in the decision to drop the Youth’s from the title. As a general interest family magazine, the Companion still censored its content to avoid references about tobacco and alcohol. It reached a circulation of 385,000 in 1885. It is also credited for being the first to print the Pledge of Allegiance, in 1892.

column3: The Cayuga Chief (1849-57) was a weekly temperance journal founded by Thurlow Weed Brown and his sister, Emma, out of Auburn, New York. He brought the journal to a circulation of around 3,000. After eight years, the Browns moved to Fort Atkinson, Wisconsin, for health reasons and a fascination for the West. The paper moved with them, and its name was changed soon after to The Wisconsin Chief, which ran from 1857-1889. Once moved, the paper seldom reached a circulation of higher than 2,000, and many of those readers were still following the paper from New York. Thurlow did most of the writing, while Emma did the printing.

Soon after its founding, Allen sold the paper to Joseph Morse and William Brewster, who had ownership of the paper until the mid 1850s. Newburyport had a population of 12,866 in 1852, and its main industries were fishing, iron, and goods manufacturing.

column3: The Long-Islander (1838-present) is a weekly newspaper founded in Huntington, New York, by nineteen-year-old Walt Whitman. Although financed by business partners, Whitman did all the writing, editing, printing, and delivering himself. This, and the fact that Whitman accepted various household goods such as potatoes as payment, led to sporadic deliveries; circulation never exceeded 200. Luckily for the longevity of the paper, Whitman sold the paper, after less than a year, to E. O. Crowell, who regularized and popularized the paper over the next ten years. In the mid-nineteenth century, Long Island was primarily agricultural with shipping and fishing industries. The railroad came through in the 1840s, connecting the region to New York City.

Goodhue became the editor in 1845 when the circulation was only 250, adding local color to the paper’s previously purely reprinted material. Readership increased. Originally, the paper had no party affiliations, but by 1847, Goodhue’s Whig support was showing through in the columns. Lancaster was a small but growing community as the seat of Grant County, with a population of 933 in 1848. Agriculture was the main industry, but there were also many mining communities surrounding the town.
